title: SQL Introduction
overview: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for talking to a relational database — reading data, writing data, and defining the structure that holds it.
It's declarative: you describe **what** result you want, and the database engine works out **how** to produce it.
Every relational database — MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server — understands the same core SQL, so the language you learn once transfers everywhere.
why: Almost every backend application talks to a relational database at some point — users, orders, products, payments — so SQL is the interface between application code and stored data.

Before declarative query languages existed, retrieving data meant writing procedural code that walked file structures by hand, record by record — tightly coupling application code to the physical layout of the data.

SQL solved that by introducing a declarative model — you state the desired result, and the engine decides the retrieval strategy — which is why SQL has barely changed in decades even as storage engines underneath it have been completely rebuilt.
Interviewers use SQL questions to check both raw syntax fluency and whether you understand what a query actually asks the database to do.
A shaky grasp of basic SQL categories (DDL vs DML vs DQL) is an easy, avoidable way to lose credibility early in an interview.

intro: SQL Introduction covers three things every developer needs before writing a single query: what kind of language SQL is, how its commands are grouped, and — at a high level — what happens when a statement is run.

topics.heading: What Is SQL?
points: SQL is a **declarative** language for relational data — you say **what** you want ("give me all active users"), not **how** to fetch it ("open this file, scan these blocks, follow this pointer").
This is the opposite of procedural/imperative code, where you specify each step: loop, compare, collect, return.
Because SQL is declarative, the same query can run correctly against a tiny table or a billion-row table — the engine decides the retrieval strategy, not the developer.
SQL operates on **relations** — tables made of rows and columns — which is why databases that use it are called relational databases.
SQL is standardized (ANSI SQL), but every vendor adds its own extensions, so exact syntax can vary slightly between MySQL, PostgreSQL, and others.

topics.heading: SQL Categories
points: **DDL (Data Definition Language)** — defines and modifies structure: `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`. Think "shape of the data," not the data itself.
**DML (Data Manipulation Language)** — changes the data inside tables: `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
**DQL (Data Query Language)** — reads data: `SELECT`. Some textbooks fold DQL into DML, but it's useful to call it out separately since it's the command used most often.
**DCL (Data Control Language)** — manages permissions: `GRANT`, `REVOKE`. Controls who is allowed to run the other categories.
**TCL (Transaction Control Language)** — manages transaction boundaries: `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`. Groups multiple statements into a single all-or-nothing unit.
Recognizing which category a keyword belongs to is a quick way to reason about its blast radius — DDL changes structure for everyone, DML changes rows, DQL changes nothing at all.

topics.heading: SQL Execution Basics
points: At a beginner level, running a SQL statement follows three broad steps: the statement is **sent** to the database, **parsed** (checked for valid syntax and that the referenced tables/columns exist), and then **executed** to produce a result.
The application (or a SQL client) sends the statement as plain text over a connection to the database server.
The database returns either a result set (for `SELECT`) or an acknowledgment of how many rows were affected (for `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`).

This page keeps execution deliberately high-level — the deeper mechanics (how the engine picks an execution plan, when indexes get used, how the plan is actually carried out) belong to query execution internals, not the SQL language itself.
See the Database section's **Query Execution** page for that deeper internals discussion — it isn't duplicated here on purpose, since this SQL section focuses only on how to *write* queries.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Developer writes a SQL statement"] --> B["Statement sent to the database"]
    B --> C["Parsed for valid syntax and existing tables/columns"]
    C --> D["Executed by the database engine"]
    D --> E["Result returned to the caller"]
    E --> F1["SELECT: rows returned"]
    E --> F2["INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE: row count returned"]
architectureFlow.caption: A bird's-eye view of what happens to a SQL statement after it leaves your application — the deep internals of parsing and execution planning live on the Database section's Query Execution page.

comparisonTables.title: SQL Categories at a Glance
rows: Transaction boundaries — when changes become permanent

simpleExample.scenario: A new engineer joins a team and is onboarded onto the `orders` database — they need read-only access, then run their first exploratory query, touching every SQL category along the way.
simpleExample.code:
CREATE TABLE customers (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100));  -- DDL
INSERT INTO customers (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Asha');            -- DML

-- DBA grants the new engineer read-only access (DCL)
GRANT SELECT ON customers TO new_engineer;

-- New engineer explores the table (DQL)
SELECT id, name FROM customers ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5;

COMMIT;  -- TCL
simpleExample.input: An empty database, plus a new database user `new_engineer` with no prior grants.
simpleExample.execution: Each statement is sent to the database independently; the engine recognizes the leading keyword (`CREATE`, `INSERT`, `GRANT`, `SELECT`, `COMMIT`) and routes it to the appropriate handling — structural change, row change, permission change, read, or transaction boundary. The `GRANT` is DCL — it changes permissions, not data or structure — so once it runs, the engineer's `SELECT` is parsed to confirm the table/columns exist and that the user has permission, then executed.
simpleExample.output: A new table exists, one row is inserted, the engineer gains read-only access, their SELECT returns the 5 newest customers, and the pending changes are made permanent.
simpleExample.explanation: The five statements map directly onto the five SQL categories — DDL creates the table shape, DML inserts data into it, DCL controls who else can read it, DQL reads it back, and TCL finalizes the change. This mirrors a common real onboarding flow: the categories aren't just academic labels, they describe real permission and blast-radius boundaries.

interviewExplanation: SQL is Structured Query Language — the standard way to talk to a relational database. The key thing I'd call out is that it's declarative: I describe what result I want, not the steps to get there. That's different from writing a loop in Java to scan a list — here the database engine decides how to actually fetch and sort the data, which is also why the same query keeps working whether the table has a hundred rows or a hundred million.

SQL commands split into five categories. DDL — CREATE, ALTER, DROP — changes structure. DML — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — changes data. DQL is just SELECT, reading data without touching it. DCL — GRANT, REVOKE — controls permissions. And TCL — COMMIT, ROLLBACK — manages transaction boundaries. I like knowing these because it tells me a statement's blast radius before I even read the details — a DDL change affects everyone, a DML change affects rows, a DQL statement changes nothing.

At a basic level, a statement gets sent to the database, parsed for syntax and that the tables/columns exist, then executed, and you get back either rows or a row count. I keep that at a high level on purpose — the deeper mechanics of how the optimizer picks a plan or when an index kicks in is really a database-internals topic, separate from just knowing how to write the query.

followUpQuestions.q: Why is SQL called a declarative language, and why does that matter?
followUpQuestions.a: Because you describe the desired result — "rows where status is ACTIVE, sorted by name" — rather than the steps to produce it. The database engine, not the developer, decides how to retrieve and sort the data. This matters because it decouples the query from the physical storage layout: the engine can change how data is stored or indexed underneath, and the same SQL still works.

followUpQuestions.q: Is SELECT part of DML or its own category?
followUpQuestions.a: Conventions differ. Many textbooks group SELECT under DML since it's still a data-facing command, but a lot of interview material — and this page — separates it out as DQL because it's read-only and never changes data, unlike INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the practical difference in impact between running a DDL statement versus a DML statement in production?
followUpQuestions.a: DDL changes structure that every user and every query depends on — dropping a column or altering a type can break other application code instantly and is hard to undo. DML changes data within existing structure, usually scoped to specific rows via WHERE — its blast radius is smaller and more predictable, though a missing WHERE clause can still affect an entire table.

followUpQuestions.q: What risk does granting broad DCL permissions introduce, and how would you scope access more safely?
followUpQuestions.a: A blanket GRANT ALL gives a user structural and data-changing power they likely don't need, increasing the damage from a mistake or a compromised credential. The safer approach is to grant the narrowest permission that satisfies the use case — e.g., SELECT-only for a reporting user — and add more only when a real need arises.

followUpQuestions.q: Do you need to understand how the database executes a query internally to write good SQL?
followUpQuestions.a: Not to get started. Writing correct SQL only requires understanding what each clause does. The deeper internals — parsing, planning, optimization, execution — matter more once you're troubleshooting performance, and that material lives separately in database internals rather than in the SQL language itself.

bestPractices: Learn to recognize a statement's category on sight — it tells you its blast radius before you even read the details.
Treat DDL and DCL statements with extra caution in production — they affect structure or access for every user, not just one row.
When exploring an unfamiliar table for the first time, reach for a narrow `SELECT` with `LIMIT` rather than pulling the whole table.
commonMistakes: Assuming `SELECT` is the only SQL command that matters and neglecting DDL/DCL/TCL vocabulary — interviewers often ask for the category names directly.
Confusing DDL with DML — for example, thinking `ALTER TABLE` changes data (it changes structure; it's DDL, not DML).
Treating SQL as if it were procedural — trying to specify *how* to fetch rows instead of simply describing *what* rows are needed.
summary: SQL is a declarative language for relational data — you state what you want, and the database figures out how to get it.
SQL commands fall into five categories: DDL (structure), DML (data), DQL (reads), DCL (permissions), and TCL (transactions).
At a basic level, a statement is sent to the database, parsed, and executed — the deep mechanics of that process belong to the Database section's Query Execution page, not here.

relatedTopics.label: Database Introduction — what a relational database actually is

title: CRUD Operations
overview: CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four core operations every application performs on stored data.
In SQL, these map directly onto four statements: `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE`.
A fifth pattern, UPSERT, combines insert-or-update into a single statement for cases where you don't know in advance whether a row already exists.
why: Applications need a consistent, predictable way to create, retrieve, change, and remove data — without that, every feature would need its own bespoke data-access logic.
SQL solves this by giving each operation its own dedicated statement with a clear, standardized syntax that works the same way across relational databases.
Virtually every feature in a real application boils down to some combination of these four operations — creating a user, reading a product catalog, updating an order status, deleting a stale session.
Interviewers use CRUD questions as a baseline check: if you can't confidently write an UPDATE with a WHERE clause, more advanced SQL topics won't matter.
Getting CRUD statements wrong in production — especially UPDATE/DELETE without a WHERE clause — is one of the most common and most damaging mistakes a developer can make.

intro: CRUD operations in SQL split into five practical patterns: the four core statements plus the UPSERT pattern that combines two of them.

topics.heading: CRUD Operations
points: **SELECT** — `SELECT col1, col2 FROM table WHERE condition ORDER BY col ASC/DESC LIMIT n OFFSET m`.
**INSERT** — `INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('John', 'john@mail.com')`. Bulk form: `INSERT INTO ... SELECT ...`.
**UPDATE** — `UPDATE users SET name = 'Jane' WHERE id = 1`. Always include a `WHERE` clause.
**DELETE** — `DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1`. `TRUNCATE TABLE users` is faster and resets auto-increment.
**UPSERT** — MySQL: `INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`. PostgreSQL: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Application needs a data change"] --> B{"Which operation?"}
    B -->|"Create a row"| C["INSERT INTO table (...) VALUES (...)"]
    B -->|"Read rows"| D["SELECT ... FROM table WHERE ..."]
    B -->|"Change existing rows"| E["UPDATE table SET ... WHERE ..."]
    B -->|"Remove rows"| F["DELETE FROM table WHERE ..."]
    B -->|"Insert or update"| G["UPSERT: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT/DUPLICATE KEY"]
architectureFlow.caption: The four CRUD operations map one-to-one onto four SQL statements, with UPSERT as a combined insert-or-update shortcut for when existence is uncertain.

simpleExample.scenario: An e-commerce backend needs to record a new order, look it up for a receipt, mark it shipped, keep a per-customer order-count summary current, and later purge cancelled orders older than a year.
simpleExample.code:
-- Create: place a new order
INSERT INTO orders (id, customer_id, status, total)
VALUES (9321, 501, 'PLACED', 249.99);

-- Read: fetch it for a receipt
SELECT id, customer_id, status, total
FROM orders
WHERE id = 9321;

-- Update: mark it shipped
UPDATE orders SET status = 'SHIPPED' WHERE id = 9321;

-- Upsert: keep an order-count summary current (PostgreSQL)
INSERT INTO customer_order_summary (customer_id, order_count)
VALUES (501, 1)
ON CONFLICT (customer_id)
DO UPDATE SET order_count = customer_order_summary.order_count + 1;

-- Delete: purge old cancelled orders
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE status = 'CANCELLED' AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '1 year';
simpleExample.input: An `orders` table used across the checkout, shipping, and admin-cleanup parts of the application, plus a `customer_order_summary` table keyed on `customer_id`.
simpleExample.execution: INSERT creates the order row. SELECT retrieves it by primary key for display. UPDATE transitions its status in place. The UPSERT either inserts a fresh summary row for a first-time customer or increments the existing count — the database decides which, based on whether `customer_id` already exists. DELETE removes only rows matching both the status and age conditions.
simpleExample.output: A new order exists and is retrievable, its status reflects shipment, the customer's order-count summary is accurate whether they're new or returning, and only genuinely old cancelled orders are removed — active and recent orders are untouched.
simpleExample.explanation: Every CRUD operation here is scoped with a WHERE clause (or an ON CONFLICT target) so it only ever touches the intended row(s) — the UPSERT specifically avoids the awkward "check if it exists, then decide whether to insert or update" round trip that would otherwise require two separate queries and risk a race condition between them.

interviewExplanation: CRUD maps directly onto SQL: Create is INSERT, Read is SELECT, Update is UPDATE, Delete is DELETE. Almost every feature in a real application is some combination of these four — creating a user, updating an order's status, deleting a stale session. If I can't confidently write an UPDATE with a WHERE clause, nothing more advanced really matters, which is why this is usually one of the first things interviewers check.

The one thing I always flag is that UPDATE and DELETE should never run without a WHERE clause — leave it off and you silently affect every row in the table. In practice I test the WHERE condition with a plain SELECT first to see exactly which rows would be hit before running the UPDATE or DELETE for real.

There's also a fifth pattern worth knowing — UPSERT — which inserts a row if it doesn't exist or updates it if it does, all in one statement. It's genuinely useful for things like keeping a running counter in sync, because it avoids a check-then-write round trip in application code that could race with another write. The syntax isn't portable though — MySQL uses ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE, PostgreSQL uses ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, and SQL Server uses MERGE.

followUpQuestions.q: Why does TRUNCATE reset auto-increment but DELETE doesn't?
followUpQuestions.a: TRUNCATE deallocates the underlying data pages entirely and reinitializes the table, including its identity counter. DELETE removes rows individually and leaves the counter untouched — that's also why DELETE is slower and TRUNCATE is effectively instant.

followUpQuestions.q: Why reach for UPSERT instead of checking existence first in application code?
followUpQuestions.a: A SELECT-then-branch approach in application code has a race window: two concurrent requests can both see "no row exists" and both try to insert, causing a duplicate-key error or a lost update. UPSERT pushes the insert-or-update decision into the database as a single atomic operation, closing that race.

followUpQuestions.q: Is UPSERT syntax the same across databases?
followUpQuestions.a: No — the concept is universal but the syntax differs: MySQL uses `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`, PostgreSQL uses `ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`, and SQL Server uses a `MERGE` statement. Naming more than one shows breadth if asked.

followUpQuestions.q: Can you undo a DELETE or UPDATE after it runs?
followUpQuestions.a: Only if it happened inside a transaction that hasn't been committed yet — a ROLLBACK can undo it. Once committed, undoing requires restoring from a backup or replaying an audit log; there's no built-in "undo" for committed CRUD statements.

followUpQuestions.q: When would TRUNCATE not be a safe substitute for DELETE, even if you want to clear an entire table?
followUpQuestions.a: When you need to keep the auto-increment counter, when row-level triggers must fire, when the table is referenced by foreign keys that block TRUNCATE, or when you need to remove only some rows rather than all — TRUNCATE offers no WHERE clause.

bestPractices: Always pair `UPDATE` and `DELETE` with a `WHERE` clause — test the matching rows with a `SELECT` first.
Use `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` for pagination instead of loading an entire table into the application.
Prefer `EXISTS`-style checks or explicit columns over `SELECT *` in production queries.
Reach for UPSERT when you genuinely don't know if a row exists yet, rather than doing a SELECT-then-branch in application code — it avoids race conditions between the check and the write.
commonMistakes: Running `UPDATE` or `DELETE` without a `WHERE` clause, silently affecting every row in the table.
Using `DELETE` when `TRUNCATE` would be both faster and semantically correct for clearing an entire table.
Assuming UPSERT syntax is portable — it is not; MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server each use different keywords for the same concept.
Forgetting that `INSERT` column order must match the `VALUES` order exactly when column names are omitted.
summary: CRUD — Create, Read, Update, Delete — maps directly onto SQL's INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements.
UPDATE and DELETE should always carry a WHERE clause to avoid silently affecting every row in the table.
UPSERT combines insert-or-update into one statement, with syntax that varies by database engine; TRUNCATE is a faster, all-or-nothing alternative to DELETE.

relatedTopics.label: Keys & Constraints — rules the database enforces on every INSERT/UPDATE

title: Filtering & Sorting
overview: Filtering and sorting let you shape a result set down to exactly the rows an application needs, in exactly the order it needs them.
Filtering happens through the `WHERE` clause and its operators; sorting happens through `ORDER BY`.
`LIMIT`/`OFFSET` build on both to slice a large result set into pages.
why: Without a way to narrow down rows, every query would return an entire table, forcing the application to filter and sort in memory after the fact.
SQL solves this by pushing filtering and sorting down to the database engine itself — closer to where the data lives — via WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT/OFFSET.
Real tables can hold millions of rows — no application wants, or can afford, to pull all of them back just to find the few that matter.

Filtering and sorting are used in nearly every query written in practice, from a simple lookup by ID to a paginated list view, and interviewers frequently probe WHERE-clause operators and pagination technique because they're both extremely common and easy to get subtly wrong.
Getting sorting and pagination right directly affects application performance and user experience — wrong ORDER BY or missing LIMIT can mean scanning and returning far more data than necessary.

intro: Filtering and sorting break down into five practical pieces: WHERE operators, the LIKE pattern-matching operator, combining conditions with AND/OR/NOT, ORDER BY, and LIMIT/OFFSET for pagination.
This section only covers how these clauses are written and what they do to the result set — not how the engine physically speeds up filtering with indexes, which belongs to the Database section.

topics.heading: Filtering & Sorting
points: **WHERE clauses** — operators like `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`, `IS NOT NULL`.
**LIKE pattern** — `%` matches any characters, `_` matches a single character. Example: `WHERE name LIKE 'J%'`.
**AND / OR / NOT** — combine conditions; use parentheses to control precedence.
**ORDER BY** — `ORDER BY name ASC, created_at DESC`. PostgreSQL adds `NULLS FIRST` / `NULLS LAST`.
**LIMIT & OFFSET** — pagination: `LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40` returns page 3 at 20 rows per page.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Full table of rows"] --> B["WHERE narrows rows by condition"]
    B --> C["ORDER BY sorts the remaining rows"]
    C --> D["LIMIT / OFFSET slices out one page"]
    D --> E["Result set returned to the application"]
architectureFlow.caption: Filtering narrows the rows first, sorting arranges what's left, and LIMIT/OFFSET carves out just the requested page — the same conceptual pipeline every filtered, sorted, paginated query follows.

comparisonTables.title: WHERE Operators at a Glance
rows: Match any value in a list

simpleExample.scenario: An admin dashboard needs page 3 of active, high-value orders — orders that are not cancelled, placed in the last 90 days, and worth more than 500, sorted by newest first.
simpleExample.code:
SELECT id, customer_id, status, total, created_at
FROM orders
WHERE status != 'CANCELLED'
  AND total > 500
  AND created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'
  AND (status = 'COMPLETED' OR status = 'SHIPPED')
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40;
simpleExample.input: An `orders` table containing orders across every status and every customer, spanning several years.
simpleExample.execution: WHERE combines multiple conditions with AND/OR: it excludes cancelled orders, requires a total above 500, requires a recent created_at, and — using parentheses to control precedence — requires the status to be either COMPLETED or SHIPPED. ORDER BY then sorts the surviving rows by created_at, newest first. LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40 skips the first 40 matches (pages 1 and 2) and returns the next 20 (page 3).
simpleExample.output: Exactly 20 rows — orders 41 through 60 in the newest-first, high-value, non-cancelled result set — ready to render as page 3 of the admin dashboard.
simpleExample.explanation: The parentheses around `(status = 'COMPLETED' OR status = 'SHIPPED')` are essential — without them, AND/OR precedence rules would group the conditions differently and silently change which rows match. This is exactly the kind of subtle WHERE-clause mistake interviewers look for.

interviewExplanation: Filtering and sorting are what turn a full table scan into exactly the rows an application needs, in the order it needs them. WHERE narrows the rows down using operators like equality, ranges, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE, and the NULL checks. ORDER BY then sorts what's left, ascending by default. LIMIT and OFFSET slice that sorted result into a page — this is the pipeline behind nearly every paginated list view I've built.

A couple of things I'm always careful about here. First, NULL — in SQL, comparing anything to NULL with equals gives an unknown result, not true, so WHERE column = NULL matches nothing; you have to use IS NULL. Second, mixing AND and OR without parentheses is a real trap — operator precedence can group conditions differently than you intended and silently change which rows come back, so I always add parentheses explicitly even when I'm fairly sure of the precedence rules.

On pagination, LIMIT/OFFSET is the simple approach, but it has a real cost at scale — a large OFFSET forces the database to scan and discard all the skipped rows before it can return the requested page, so performance degrades as you paginate deeper. For very large tables I'd bring up keyset or cursor-based pagination — filtering on the last-seen value instead of an offset — as the more scalable alternative.

followUpQuestions.q: Why doesn't WHERE column = NULL work to find NULL values?
followUpQuestions.a: In SQL, NULL represents an unknown value, and comparing anything to an unknown with = yields an unknown result — not true — so the row never matches. Use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to check for NULL explicitly.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the difference between ORDER BY column ASC and just ORDER BY column?
followUpQuestions.a: They're equivalent — ASC (ascending) is the default sort direction if none is specified. DESC must be stated explicitly to sort in descending order.

followUpQuestions.q: Is LIMIT/OFFSET the only way to paginate results?
followUpQuestions.a: No — it's the simplest and most common approach, but keyset/cursor-based pagination (filtering on the last seen value instead of using OFFSET) scales better for very large tables since it avoids scanning and discarding rows to reach a deep page.

followUpQuestions.q: Why does a query with a large OFFSET get slower as the offset grows?
followUpQuestions.a: The database still has to scan through all the skipped rows in sorted order before it can start returning the requested page — it can't jump straight to row 10,000 without passing through the first 9,999 first. That scan cost grows linearly with the offset, which is why deep pagination on a big table gets progressively slower.

followUpQuestions.q: How would you write a WHERE clause to safely filter on a nullable column while still including rows where that column is NULL?
followUpQuestions.a: Combine the condition with an explicit NULL check using OR, wrapped in parentheses — for example `WHERE (status = 'ACTIVE' OR status IS NULL)` — since a plain equality check alone would silently exclude every row where the column is NULL.

bestPractices: Wrap mixed AND/OR conditions in parentheses explicitly — never rely on remembering operator precedence rules under pressure.
Use `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` for pagination instead of loading an entire table into the application and slicing it in memory.
Prefer `IN (...)` over a long chain of `OR` conditions on the same column — it's easier to read and easier to maintain.
Be explicit about `NULL` handling — remember that `= NULL` never matches; use `IS NULL` instead.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that `NULL` is not equal to anything, including itself — `WHERE column = NULL` silently matches zero rows; `IS NULL` is required.
Mixing AND and OR without parentheses, causing the condition to group differently than intended.
Using a large `OFFSET` for deep pagination on a big table, which forces the database to scan and discard many rows before reaching the requested page.
Forgetting that `LIKE` wildcards are `%` and `_` in SQL, not `*` and `?` as in shell globbing.
summary: WHERE filters individual rows using operators like =, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE, and IS NULL, optionally combined with AND/OR/NOT.
ORDER BY sorts the surviving rows, ascending by default or descending with DESC, and can sort by multiple columns.
LIMIT/OFFSET slices a sorted, filtered result set into pages, but parentheses matter when mixing AND and OR — they control which conditions get grouped together.

relatedTopics.label: Indexing — why a WHERE clause can be instant or slow

title: Aggregate Functions
overview: Aggregate functions collapse many rows into a single summary value — a total, an average, a count.
SQL provides five core ones: `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MIN()`, `MAX()`.
Each behaves slightly differently around `NULL` values, which is one of the most common places this topic trips people up.
`DISTINCT` isn't an aggregate function itself, but it's used constantly alongside them to dedupe before counting or summarizing.
why: Almost every dashboard, invoice, or report question is really an aggregate question — "how many," "how much," "what's the average."
Without these functions, an application would have to pull every raw row over the network and do the math in code, which is slower and wastes bandwidth.
SQL pushes that computation down into the database engine, which can compute a sum or count far more efficiently than the application layer.
Interviewers use `COUNT(*)` vs `COUNT(column)` as a quick, reliable check for whether a candidate understands how NULLs actually behave in aggregation.
These functions are also the foundation `GROUP BY` and `HAVING` build on — you can't reason about grouping until you know what's being aggregated per group.

intro: Aggregate functions all follow the same basic contract — take a set of rows, return one value — but they differ in exactly how they treat `NULL` and duplicate values.

topics.heading: The Five Core Aggregate Functions
points: **`COUNT()`** — counts rows. `COUNT(*)` counts every row in the set, NULLs included. `COUNT(column)` counts only rows where that column is not `NULL`.
**`SUM()`** — adds up a numeric column. `NULL` values are ignored, not treated as zero; summing an all-`NULL` column returns `NULL`, not `0`.
**`AVG()`** — computes an average. Also ignores `NULL`s in both the total and the row count used for the division — it does **not** treat missing values as zero.
**`MIN()` / `MAX()`** — return the smallest/largest value in a column, working on numbers, strings, and dates alike. `NULL`s are excluded from consideration.
All five can run over an entire table with no `GROUP BY` at all — in that case the whole table is treated as one implicit group, returning a single summary row.

topics.heading: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) vs COUNT(DISTINCT column)
points: `COUNT(*)` is a row count — it doesn't look at any column's values, so `NULL`s never exclude a row.
`COUNT(column)` looks at that specific column's values and skips any row where it's `NULL` — this is the number one source of "why is my count different" confusion.
`COUNT(DISTINCT column)` counts only the unique non-`NULL` values in that column, deduping before counting.
These three can legitimately return three different numbers on the exact same table — that difference is the point, not a bug.

topics.heading: DISTINCT
points: `SELECT DISTINCT department FROM employees` removes duplicate rows from the result — a form of deduplication rather than aggregation.
`DISTINCT` pairs naturally with aggregates: `COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id)` answers "how many unique customers," not "how many rows."
`DISTINCT` applies to the whole selected row (or the whole expression it wraps) — with multiple columns, it dedupes on the combination of all of them, not each column independently.

simpleExample.scenario: Summarize an `orders` table — total order count, total revenue, average order value, and the cheapest and priciest order.
simpleExample.code:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
  COUNT(discount_code) AS orders_with_discount,
  SUM(total) AS total_revenue,
  AVG(total) AS avg_order_value,
  MIN(total) AS cheapest_order,
  MAX(total) AS priciest_order
FROM orders;
simpleExample.input: An `orders` table where the `discount_code` column is `NULL` for orders that didn't use a discount.
simpleExample.execution: COUNT(*) counts every row regardless of NULLs. COUNT(discount_code) counts only rows where a discount code was actually used, since NULL rows are skipped. SUM and AVG add up and average the total column, ignoring any NULL totals. MIN and MAX scan the same column for the smallest and largest values.
simpleExample.output: A single summary row: total order count, a smaller count of only discounted orders, total revenue, the average order value, and the cheapest/priciest order amounts.
simpleExample.explanation: This is aggregation with no GROUP BY at all — the whole table is treated as one group, so every function returns exactly one value across all rows. COUNT(*) and COUNT(discount_code) differing is the clearest illustration of NULL-handling in this page.

interviewExplanation: Aggregate functions take a bunch of rows and boil them down into one number — count, sum, average, min, max. The thing that trips people up is NULL handling: COUNT(*) counts rows no matter what, but COUNT(column) only counts rows where that column actually has a value. SUM and AVG both just skip NULLs entirely — they don't treat them as zero, which matters a lot if you're averaging a column that's got some missing data in it.

If someone asks me to count unique customers instead of unique orders, that's COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) — DISTINCT dedupes the values before COUNT ever sees them. And all of this works even without a GROUP BY — if you just run SUM(total) on a whole table with no grouping, the engine treats the entire table as one big implicit group and hands back a single row.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column_name)?
followUpQuestions.a: COUNT(*) counts every row in the group regardless of NULL values anywhere. COUNT(column_name) counts only rows where that specific column is not NULL — the two can return different numbers on the exact same data if that column has any NULLs in it.

followUpQuestions.q: Does SUM() or AVG() treat NULL as zero?
followUpQuestions.a: No. Both functions simply ignore NULL values — they're excluded from the total and, for AVG, excluded from the row count used in the division too. Summing a column that's entirely NULL returns NULL, not 0, which surprises people who expect zero as a safe default.

followUpQuestions.q: What does COUNT(DISTINCT column) do differently from COUNT(column)?
followUpQuestions.a: COUNT(column) counts every non-NULL row, including duplicates. COUNT(DISTINCT column) first dedupes the non-NULL values and then counts only the unique ones — useful for questions like "how many unique customers placed an order" versus "how many orders were placed."

followUpQuestions.q: Can you use an aggregate function without GROUP BY?
followUpQuestions.a: Yes — with no GROUP BY, the database treats the entire result set as a single implicit group, so an aggregate function computes one value across all matching rows and returns exactly one row.

followUpQuestions.q: Do MIN() and MAX() only work on numbers?
followUpQuestions.a: No — they work on any orderable type, including strings (alphabetical comparison) and dates (chronological comparison), not just numeric columns.

followUpQuestions.q: Is DISTINCT itself an aggregate function?
followUpQuestions.a: No — DISTINCT is a deduplication modifier, not an aggregate function. It removes duplicate rows from a result set but doesn't compute a summary value the way COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, or MAX do. It's often combined with COUNT, as in COUNT(DISTINCT column), but it can also stand alone in a plain SELECT DISTINCT.

bestPractices: Use `COUNT(*)` when you want a plain row count, and `COUNT(column)` only when you specifically need to exclude `NULL`s in that column.
Don't assume `SUM()`/`AVG()` of a nullable column silently defaults missing values to zero — check whether that's actually the behavior you want, and use `COALESCE()` if it isn't.
Reach for `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` when the question is about unique entities, not row volume.
Test aggregate expressions on a small, known slice of data first so you can confirm NULL-handling behaves the way you expect before trusting it at scale.
commonMistakes: Assuming `COUNT(*)` and `COUNT(column)` always return the same number — they diverge as soon as that column has any `NULL`s.
Expecting `SUM()` of an all-`NULL` column to return `0` — it returns `NULL`.
Forgetting that `AVG()` divides only by the count of non-`NULL` rows, not the total row count — this changes the result compared to manually summing and dividing by row count in application code.
Using `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` when a plain `COUNT(column)` was intended (or vice versa), silently changing what the number actually represents.
summary: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, and `MAX` each collapse a set of rows into a single summary value, with NULLs excluded rather than treated as zero.
`COUNT(*)` counts rows; `COUNT(column)` counts non-NULL values in that column; `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` counts unique non-NULL values.
All of these work even with no `GROUP BY` — the whole table is treated as one implicit group.

relatedTopics.label: GROUP BY & HAVING — bucketing rows before aggregating them

title: GROUP BY & HAVING
overview: `GROUP BY` buckets rows that share a value into groups, so an aggregate function computes one result per bucket instead of one result for the whole table.
`HAVING` filters those groups **after** they've been formed — it's the `WHERE` clause's counterpart for aggregate values.
The reason `HAVING` exists at all comes down to timing: a query has a strict logical execution order, and aggregates simply don't exist yet at the point `WHERE` runs.
why: Most reporting questions aren't about the whole table — they're about totals *per category*, *per customer*, *per day*.
`GROUP BY` is how SQL expresses "summarize this, but separately for each distinct value of that column."
`HAVING` lets you filter on the result of that summarization — like "only categories over ₹100,000 in revenue" — in the same declarative query, with no second pass needed.
Interviewers lean on this topic heavily because it exposes whether a candidate actually understands SQL's logical execution order, not just its written order.
Getting `WHERE` and `HAVING` mixed up is one of the most common — and most revealing — mistakes candidates make live during a SQL interview.

intro: GROUP BY and HAVING work together as a two-stage pipeline: first form the buckets, then optionally throw some buckets away based on their aggregate values.

topics.heading: GROUP BY
points: `SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM emp GROUP BY dept` buckets rows by `dept` and computes the aggregate once per bucket, not once per row.

Every non-aggregate column that appears in `SELECT` must also appear in `GROUP BY` — otherwise the database can't tell which row's value to show for a column that has multiple values within the same group.
You can group by more than one column at once — `GROUP BY category, status` creates one bucket per unique combination of the two.
`GROUP BY` can also bucket by an expression, not just a raw column — e.g. `GROUP BY DATE(created_at)` to group timestamps into calendar days.

topics.heading: HAVING
points: `SELECT dept, AVG(salary) FROM emp GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000` filters out groups whose average salary doesn't clear the bar.
`HAVING` runs strictly after `GROUP BY`, so it's the only clause that can reference an aggregate function like `SUM()` or `COUNT()` directly in its condition.
`HAVING` can be used even without an explicit `GROUP BY` — the whole result set is then treated as one implicit group, and `HAVING` filters that single group.
A plain row-level condition (like `status = 'ACTIVE'`) belongs in `WHERE`, not `HAVING` — using `HAVING` for it still works but wastes effort grouping rows that could have been discarded earlier.

topics.heading: WHERE vs HAVING — Why Both Exist
points: `WHERE` filters individual raw rows, and it runs **before** grouping — at that point in the query, no aggregate value has been computed yet.
`HAVING` filters groups, and it runs **after** grouping — by then, aggregate values like `SUM()` or `AVG()` exist and can be compared.
Trying to write `WHERE COUNT(*) > 5` fails for exactly this reason: `COUNT(*)` doesn't exist at the point `WHERE` is evaluated.
The two clauses aren't interchangeable shortcuts for each other — they operate on two different "shapes" of data at two different stages of the same query.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["FROM - pick source tables"] --> B["WHERE - filter rows"]
    B --> C["GROUP BY - form groups"]
    C --> D["HAVING - filter groups"]
    D --> E["SELECT - pick columns/expressions"]
    E --> F["ORDER BY - sort result"]
    F --> G["LIMIT / OFFSET - page result"]
architectureFlow.caption: The database evaluates a query in this logical order, not the order it's typed in. This is exactly why HAVING can filter on an aggregate but WHERE cannot: the aggregate value doesn't exist until GROUP BY has already run.

simpleExample.scenario: A dashboard needs total revenue per product category, but only for categories generating over ₹100,000, among completed orders.
simpleExample.code:
SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(total) AS revenue
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'COMPLETED'
GROUP BY category
HAVING SUM(total) > 100000
ORDER BY revenue DESC;
simpleExample.input: An `orders` table with `category`, `status`, and `total` columns across all customers.
simpleExample.execution: WHERE keeps only completed orders before anything is grouped. GROUP BY then buckets those surviving rows by category and computes COUNT(*) and SUM(total) per bucket. HAVING drops any category bucket whose summed revenue doesn't exceed 100000. Finally, ORDER BY sorts the remaining categories by revenue.
simpleExample.output: A short list of high-performing categories with their order count and total revenue, highest first.
simpleExample.explanation: HAVING is essential here because the filter (SUM(total) > 100000) depends on an aggregate that doesn't exist until after grouping — WHERE physically cannot express that condition, since it runs earlier in the logical execution order.

interviewExplanation: GROUP BY takes rows that share a value and buckets them, so instead of getting one row per order, you get one row per category, or per customer, or whatever you grouped on — with an aggregate computed for each bucket. HAVING then filters those buckets, not the original rows. So if I want categories with revenue over 100,000, I group by category, sum the total per group, and then HAVING drops any group that doesn't clear that bar.

The reason HAVING has to be a separate clause from WHERE comes down to when each one runs. SQL has a logical execution order — FROM, then WHERE, then GROUP BY, then HAVING, then SELECT, then ORDER BY, then LIMIT — and WHERE runs before GROUP BY even happens. So at the point WHERE is evaluated, there's no SUM or COUNT to compare against yet — it just doesn't exist. HAVING runs after grouping, so by then the aggregate values are real numbers you can filter on.

followUpQuestions.q: Why can't WHERE filter on an aggregate function?
followUpQuestions.a: Because of execution order — WHERE runs before GROUP BY in the query's logical execution order (FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT). At the point WHERE is evaluated, rows haven't been grouped yet, so an aggregate like SUM() or COUNT() has no value to compare against. HAVING exists specifically because it runs after grouping, once aggregates are actual computed numbers.

followUpQuestions.q: Can you use HAVING without GROUP BY?
followUpQuestions.a: Yes — without GROUP BY, the entire result set is treated as a single implicit group, so HAVING can filter based on an aggregate computed over all rows, like HAVING COUNT(*) > 100.

followUpQuestions.q: Does every column in SELECT need to be in GROUP BY?
followUpQuestions.a: Every non-aggregated column does. If a column isn't wrapped in an aggregate function and isn't listed in GROUP BY, the database can't determine a single value to display for it within a group that may contain several different values for that column — most databases will reject the query outright.

followUpQuestions.q: Can you group by more than one column?
followUpQuestions.a: Yes — GROUP BY category, status creates one bucket per unique combination of category and status, not one bucket per category and a separate one per status. The aggregate is then computed per combination.

followUpQuestions.q: Is it ever correct to put a plain row-level condition in HAVING instead of WHERE?
followUpQuestions.a: It will work, since HAVING can filter on non-aggregate columns too, but it's not efficient — the database groups every row first and only discards some groups afterward. WHERE removes non-matching rows before grouping ever starts, which is cheaper. HAVING should be reserved for conditions that genuinely depend on an aggregate value.

followUpQuestions.q: Can GROUP BY bucket on something other than a raw column?
followUpQuestions.a: Yes — you can group by an expression, such as GROUP BY DATE(created_at) to bucket timestamps into calendar days, or GROUP BY YEAR(created_at) to bucket into years. The expression just needs to produce a consistent grouping key per row.

bestPractices: Use `WHERE` to eliminate rows as early as possible — it's cheaper than grouping unnecessary rows only to discard them later with `HAVING`.
Only use `HAVING` when the filter condition genuinely depends on an aggregate function; otherwise a `WHERE` clause is simpler and faster.
List every non-aggregated `SELECT` column in `GROUP BY` to avoid ambiguous or database-specific behavior.
When grouping by an expression (like a truncated date), consider whether an equivalent generated/computed column would make the query easier to read and index.
Recite the logical execution order — FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT — until it's automatic; it explains almost every WHERE/HAVING mixup.
commonMistakes: Trying to filter on an aggregate inside `WHERE` instead of `HAVING` (e.g. `WHERE COUNT(*) > 5`), which fails because the aggregate isn't computed yet at that stage.
Forgetting that every non-aggregated column in `SELECT` must also appear in `GROUP BY`.
Using `HAVING` for a plain row-level condition that `WHERE` could handle more efficiently before grouping even begins.
Assuming `GROUP BY` preserves the original row order — it doesn't; use `ORDER BY` explicitly if a specific order is required.
summary: `GROUP BY` buckets rows sharing a value so an aggregate can be computed once per bucket instead of once per row.
`HAVING` filters those computed buckets, and can reference aggregate functions precisely because it runs after `GROUP BY`.
`WHERE` and `HAVING` operate at two different stages of the same query — WHERE before grouping, HAVING after — which is the entire reason both clauses need to exist.

relatedTopics.label: Aggregate Functions — the functions HAVING filters on
Window Functions — summarizing data without collapsing rows

title: Joins
overview: Real data is rarely in one table — orders reference customers, customers reference addresses.
**Joins** combine rows across multiple tables based on a matching column.
The join type (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER, CROSS) determines exactly which rows survive when a match is missing on one side.
why: Normalized databases split data into separate tables to avoid duplication, but an application usually needs a combined view — an order along with the customer who placed it.

Without joins, combining two tables would require fetching each separately and merging them in application code — pulling far more data over the network than necessary and pushing relational logic into the app layer.

Joins let the database do the matching internally, using indexes, and return only the combined rows actually needed — reassembling split data back into a single, useful result entirely inside the database engine.

Join questions are a staple of SQL interviews because they test whether you understand not just syntax but the consequences of a missing match — which rows survive, which get NULLs, and which disappear entirely.

intro: Joins fall into a handful of types that differ only in one thing: what happens to a row that has no match on the other side.

topics.heading: JOIN Types
points: **INNER JOIN** — returns only matching rows from both tables: `SELECT * FROM orders o INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id`.
**LEFT JOIN** — all rows from the left table, plus matches from the right; `NULL` where there's no match.
**RIGHT JOIN** — all rows from the right table, plus matches from the left. Less commonly used.
**FULL OUTER JOIN** — all rows from both tables, `NULL` where there's no match. Not supported in MySQL.
**CROSS JOIN** — a Cartesian product; every row of A paired with every row of B. Rarely needed.

topics.heading: Self Join & Multi-Table
points: **Self join** — a table joined with itself: `SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager FROM emp e JOIN emp m ON e.manager_id = m.id`.
**Multiple joins** — chain joins across tables: `FROM orders o JOIN users u ON ... JOIN products p ON ...`.
**Join with aggregation** — `SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id GROUP BY u.name`.
**Natural join** — joins automatically on columns with the same name. Implicit and fragile — avoid in production.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Read next row from left table"] --> B{"Matching row in right table?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Combine columns from both rows"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Join type is LEFT/FULL?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Combine with NULLs for right side"]
    D -- "No - INNER" --> F["Skip this row entirely"]
    C --> G["Add to result set"]
    E --> G
architectureFlow.caption: How a join decides, row by row, whether to include a match, fill with NULLs, or skip entirely — the difference between INNER and LEFT/FULL joins.

comparisonTables.title: INNER vs LEFT vs RIGHT vs FULL OUTER JOIN
rows: Only matching rows from both tables
All left rows + matches from right
All right rows + matches from left

simpleExample.scenario: A reporting query needs every customer, including those who've never ordered, along with their total order count — starting from a simple customer-to-order lookup and then switching join types to reveal inactive customers.
simpleExample.code:
-- Every order paired with the customer who placed it (INNER JOIN)
SELECT c.name, o.product_name
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id;

-- Every customer, including those with zero orders (LEFT JOIN)
SELECT c.name, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.name
HAVING COUNT(o.id) = 0;
simpleExample.input: A `customers` table and an `orders` table linked by `customer_id`.
simpleExample.execution: The first query matches each `orders` row to its `customers` row via `customer_id = c.id`, combining the two into one row per match — customers with zero orders are left out entirely. The second query switches to LEFT JOIN, which keeps every customer row even with no matching order (filling order columns with NULL), then HAVING filters down to customers with zero matched orders.
simpleExample.output: The first query returns one row per order, showing the customer's name next to the product they bought. The second returns a list of customers who have never placed an order — useful for a re-engagement campaign.
simpleExample.explanation: Switching from INNER to LEFT JOIN is what makes customers with no orders visible at all — an INNER JOIN would have silently excluded them. This is the core join lesson: the join type decides whether an unmatched row survives, gets NULLs, or disappears.

interviewExplanation: Joins combine rows across tables using a matching column, since normalized databases split related data — customers, orders, products — into separate tables to avoid duplication. The type of join is really just a rule for what happens when a row has no match on the other side. INNER JOIN keeps only rows that match on both sides. LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table regardless, filling in NULLs when there's no match on the right. RIGHT JOIN is the mirror image, and FULL OUTER JOIN keeps everything from both sides.

The way I think about it row by row: for each row on the left, the engine checks for a match on the right. If there's a match, it combines the columns. If there isn't, an INNER JOIN just skips that row, while a LEFT or FULL join still includes it with NULLs on the right side. That's really the entire distinction between join types.

A gotcha I watch for is row multiplication — if the right table has multiple matches for one left row, a join produces one output row per match, which can inflate counts unexpectedly, especially right before a GROUP BY. I'd also mention that MySQL doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN natively, so you'd simulate it with a LEFT JOIN unioned with a RIGHT JOIN.

followUpQuestions.q: Why might a LEFT JOIN return more rows than the left table has on its own?
followUpQuestions.a: If the right table has multiple matching rows for one left row, the join produces one output row per match — this can multiply row counts unexpectedly, especially before a GROUP BY. Wrapping the query with GROUP BY (and an appropriate aggregate) is the usual fix when you need one row per left-side entity.

followUpQuestions.q: When would you actually need a CROSS JOIN?
followUpQuestions.a: Rarely — mainly for generating all possible combinations, like pairing every size with every color for a product variant matrix. Outside of that kind of combinatorial generation, an unintentional CROSS JOIN (usually from a missing ON condition) is a bug, not a feature.

followUpQuestions.q: How do you get a FULL OUTER JOIN in MySQL?
followUpQuestions.a: MySQL doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN directly. Simulate it with a LEFT JOIN combined with a UNION against a RIGHT JOIN, so unmatched rows from both sides are captured.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the risk of using a natural join in a production query?
followUpQuestions.a: A natural join matches implicitly on any columns that share a name across the two tables — it isn't explicit about which columns drive the match. If an unrelated column happens to share a name (or a new column is added later that collides), the join silently changes behavior without any error, which makes it fragile and hard to debug.

followUpQuestions.q: How does the presence or absence of an index on the join column affect performance?
followUpQuestions.a: Without an index on the join column, the engine typically has to scan the entire table on that side to find matches for each row, which is expensive as tables grow. An index lets it look up matches directly, which is why join columns — especially foreign keys — are prime candidates for indexing.

bestPractices: Always specify the join condition explicitly with `ON` — never rely on implicit natural joins in production.
Use `LEFT JOIN` (not `INNER JOIN`) whenever unmatched rows on the left side still need to appear in the result.
Watch for row multiplication: if the right table has multiple matches per left row, wrap the query with `GROUP BY` when you need one row per left-side entity.
commonMistakes: Using INNER JOIN when LEFT JOIN was needed, silently dropping rows with no match.
Forgetting that NULL values appear on the unmatched side of a LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN, and not accounting for them in later filters or aggregates.
Relying on natural joins, which break silently if an unrelated column happens to share a name across tables.
summary: Joins combine rows across tables using a matching column; the type (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL) determines which unmatched rows survive.
INNER JOIN keeps only matches, LEFT/RIGHT JOIN preserve one full side with NULLs on the other, and FULL OUTER JOIN preserves both sides.
Self joins and multi-table joins apply the same matching logic, just against the same table or across more than two tables at once.

relatedTopics.label: Database Design — the relationships joins reassemble

title: Subqueries
overview: A **subquery** nests one query inside another to answer a question that needs an intermediate result first.
It can return a single value, a list of values, or simply prove whether any matching rows exist at all.
Subqueries appear inside `SELECT`, `WHERE`, or `FROM`, letting you build up a result in layers rather than one flat query.
why: Some questions can't be answered by filtering on a table's own columns alone — they depend on data computed from a related table or another slice of the same table.

An outer query sometimes needs a value that only exists after examining that other data first; fetching it in a separate round trip means an extra network call and extra application-side code to stitch the results together.

A subquery lets you compute that related value or condition first, then use it to filter or shape the outer query — the database resolves everything in one pass, staying declarative instead of leaking logic into the application.
This is exactly the gap subqueries close: they let one query depend on the result of another, without the caller ever seeing two separate round trips.

intro: Subqueries come in a few distinct shapes, depending on what they return and whether they depend on the outer query.

topics.heading: Subqueries
points: **Scalar subquery** — returns a single value: `SELECT name, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE user_id = u.id) FROM users u`.
**IN subquery** — `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders WHERE total > 100)`.
**EXISTS** — `SELECT * FROM users u WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = u.id)`. Usually faster than `IN`.
**Correlated subquery** — references the outer query and executes once per outer row; consider a JOIN instead.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Outer query starts evaluating a row"] --> B{"Subquery type?"}
    B -- "Scalar" --> C["Run subquery once, get single value"]
    B -- "IN" --> D["Run subquery once, get full list"]
    B -- "EXISTS / Correlated" --> E["Run subquery per outer row, using outer row's values"]
    C --> F["Use value to filter or project outer row"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Move to next outer row"]
architectureFlow.caption: Non-correlated subqueries (scalar, IN) typically run once regardless of outer row count; correlated subqueries and EXISTS checks are evaluated per outer row, which is why their cost scales differently.

comparisonTables.title: IN vs EXISTS
rows: Not affected by NULLs in subquery

comparisonTables.title: Subquery vs JOIN vs CTE
rows: Can nest deeply, harder to read
Breaking complex logic into named steps

simpleExample.scenario: Find every customer who has placed at least one order over 100.
simpleExample.input: A `customers` table and an `orders` table linked by `customer_id`.
simpleExample.execution: The inner query first produces the full list of `customer_id`s with a high-value order; the outer query then keeps only customers whose `id` appears in that list.
simpleExample.output: The names of customers who have at least one order over 100.
simpleExample.explanation: The IN subquery runs once and produces a fixed list — the outer query just checks membership against it, no per-row re-execution needed. Contrast this with a correlated version of the same idea, like `WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.id AND o.total > 100)`: that form is conceptually re-run once per outer row since it references the outer row's `id`, which is exactly the trade-off interviewers probe when they ask you to pick between IN and EXISTS.

interviewExplanation: A subquery is just a query inside another query, used when the outer query needs a value or a set of values that only exists after looking at some other data first. I'd reach for a scalar subquery when I need exactly one value — like a per-row order count — an IN subquery when I need to check membership against a list, and EXISTS when I only care whether a match exists at all, not what it is.

The distinction I always call out is correlated versus non-correlated. A non-correlated subquery, like a plain IN list, runs once and produces a fixed result the outer query checks against. A correlated subquery references a column from the outer row, so conceptually it re-runs for every outer row — which is fine at small scale but is usually the first thing I'd look at rewriting as a JOIN if a query is slow.

The other thing I keep in mind is the NOT IN plus NULL gotcha — if the subquery in a NOT IN can return NULL, the whole condition can silently evaluate to no rows, so I default to NOT EXISTS for absence checks instead.

followUpQuestions.q: Why do subqueries exist when JOINs already combine tables?
followUpQuestions.a: A JOIN combines full row sets from two tables side by side. A subquery is for when you only need a derived value or a yes/no check from another table or slice of data — not the actual joined columns. Forcing every such case into a JOIN often means an extra GROUP BY and more columns than you actually need just to get one number.

followUpQuestions.q: What happens internally when a correlated subquery runs?
followUpQuestions.a: Conceptually, the database re-evaluates the inner query once per row produced by the outer query, substituting that row's values into the correlation condition each time. Modern optimizers often rewrite this into a semi-join or anti-join internally so it doesn't literally execute row by row, but you should reason about correctness as if it does — performance is the optimizer's job, not a guarantee.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the advantage of a subquery over running two separate queries in application code?
followUpQuestions.a: It keeps the whole computation inside the database in one round trip and one execution plan, instead of one query, a network hop back to the app, application logic to extract values, and a second query. It's also easier for the database to optimize a single statement than for you to hand-optimize two separate ones.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the main disadvantage or risk of subqueries?
followUpQuestions.a: Deep nesting hurts readability fast, and correlated subqueries can silently turn into a per-row cost that only shows up under load. There's also the NOT IN plus NULL trap: if the subquery can return NULL, NOT IN can silently return zero rows instead of erroring, which is a correctness bug, not just a performance one.

followUpQuestions.q: When would you avoid a subquery entirely?
followUpQuestions.a: When you actually need columns from both tables in the final result — that's a JOIN's job. Also when a correlated subquery is doing a lookup that a window function could do instead, like comparing a row to its group's average — AVG() OVER (PARTITION BY ...) computes that once per group instead of once per row.

followUpQuestions.q: IN vs EXISTS — when do you pick one over the other, and why?
followUpQuestions.a: EXISTS short-circuits on the first match and isn't affected by NULLs in the subquery result, so I default to it for large subqueries and for absence checks (NOT EXISTS). IN is fine and often more readable for small, static lists, but I'd avoid NOT IN specifically if there's any chance the subquery result contains NULL.

bestPractices: Prefer `EXISTS` over `IN` when the subquery returns a large result set.
Consider rewriting a correlated subquery as a JOIN when it's re-evaluated for every outer row and performance suffers.
Use a scalar subquery in `SELECT` only for cheap, indexed lookups — an expensive scalar subquery runs once per outer row.
commonMistakes: Writing a correlated subquery that re-executes per outer row when a JOIN would run once and be faster.
Forgetting that NULL values inside an IN subquery's result can cause the entire condition to evaluate as unknown.
Using IN with a subquery that can return NULL, instead of NOT EXISTS, when checking for absence — NOT IN with NULLs in the list can silently return zero rows.
summary: Subqueries nest a query inside another to compute an intermediate value, check existence, or filter by a related result.
Scalar subqueries return one value, IN subqueries return a list to check membership against, and EXISTS/correlated subqueries test row-by-row conditions.
EXISTS generally outperforms IN on large subqueries, and correlated subqueries are often better rewritten as JOINs for performance.

relatedTopics.label: Common Table Expressions — naming a subquery for readability and reuse

title: Common Table Expressions
overview: A Common Table Expression (`CTE`) is a named, temporary result set defined with a `WITH` clause that you can reference like a table inside a single `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` statement.
CTEs come in two flavors: **non-recursive** (a readable stand-in for a subquery) and **recursive** (a CTE that refers to itself to walk hierarchical or graph-like data).
Unlike a view, a CTE is not saved in the database — it exists only for the duration of the query that defines it.
Recursive CTEs are the standard SQL tool for problems that would otherwise require application-side loops: org charts, category trees, bill-of-materials explosions, and graph traversal.
why: Complex queries built from nested subqueries become unreadable fast, and the same subquery often gets duplicated if it's needed in more than one place — a CTE lets you name each logical step and read the query top to bottom.

Hierarchical data (managers and reports, folders and subfolders, categories and subcategories) cannot be unwound with a fixed number of joins because the depth isn't known in advance — recursive CTEs handle arbitrary depth.

Before CTEs, developers solved hierarchy traversal with application code — query the top-level row, then loop and issue a new query per level, which is slow and chatty — or with proprietary syntax like Oracle's `CONNECT BY` that didn't port across engines.
CTEs make multi-step ETL-style logic (filter, then aggregate, then rank) expressible in a single statement instead of temporary tables.
Interviewers use recursive CTEs specifically to test whether a candidate understands iteration expressed declaratively, which is a common gap for developers coming from procedural languages.

intro: A CTE is introduced with `WITH cte_name AS (subquery)` placed before the main statement; recursive CTEs add the keyword `RECURSIVE` and a self-reference inside the CTE body.

topics.heading: Non-Recursive CTEs
points: Syntax: `WITH cte_name (col1, col2) AS (SELECT ...) SELECT * FROM cte_name;` — the column list is optional if the inner `SELECT` already has clear column names.

Its only job is to give a subquery a name so the outer query reads like plain English: `WITH high_value_orders AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 1000) SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM high_value_orders GROUP BY customer_id;`
Multiple CTEs can be chained in one `WITH` clause, each separated by a comma, and later CTEs can reference earlier ones: `WITH a AS (...), b AS (SELECT * FROM a WHERE ...) SELECT * FROM b;`
A CTE is scoped only to the statement it's attached to — it disappears once that statement finishes, unlike a view which persists in the schema.

Most engines treat a non-recursive CTE as an optimization fence or inline it like a subquery depending on the query planner — behavior differs by database, so don't assume it's always materialized once and reused for free.
Because a CTE is just a named subquery, it can be filtered, joined, or aggregated in the outer query exactly like a real table.

topics.heading: Recursive CTEs
points: Structure: a **base case** (an anchor `SELECT` with no self-reference) `UNION ALL` a **recursive case** (a `SELECT` that references the CTE's own name), wrapped in `WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS (base UNION ALL recursive)`.
The base case runs exactly once and seeds the starting rows — for example, the one employee row matching a given `manager_id`.
The recursive case then re-executes repeatedly, each time joining the CTE to itself using only the rows produced in the previous iteration, not the entire accumulated result.

The classic use case is a hierarchy: an `employees` table with a self-referencing `manager_id` column pointing to `employees.id`, used to walk down the management chain (find all reports of a manager) or up it (find all managers above an employee).
The same pattern works for any parent/child structure, such as a `categories` table with a `parent_id` column for a product category tree, or a `parts` table for a bill-of-materials explosion.
The recursion stops automatically the moment the recursive case's `SELECT` returns zero new rows for that iteration — there is no explicit loop counter to manage.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Base case SELECT runs once"] --> B["Seed rows added to result set"]
    B --> C["Recursive case joins CTE name against seed rows"]
    C --> D{"Recursive case returned new rows?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Append new rows to result set"]
    E --> F["Recursive case re-runs using only the newest rows"]
    F --> D
    D -- "No" --> G["Recursion stops, full result set returned"]
architectureFlow.caption: The recursive CTE execution model: the base case seeds the first generation, then each recursive pass joins only against the previous pass's new rows until a pass produces nothing new.

comparisonTables.title: CTE vs Subquery vs Temporary Table vs View

simpleExample.scenario: Given an `employees` table with columns `id`, `name`, and `manager_id` (self-referencing `employees.id`), find every employee who reports up to a given manager, directly or indirectly, along with how many levels down they are.
simpleExample.code:
WITH RECURSIVE reports AS (
    -- Base case: the manager's direct reports
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 1 AS depth
    FROM employees
    WHERE manager_id = 101

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive case: reports of the people already found
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, r.depth + 1
    FROM employees e
    INNER JOIN reports r ON e.manager_id = r.id
)
SELECT id, name, depth
FROM reports
ORDER BY depth, name;
simpleExample.input:
employees table:
id=101 name='Aria (VP)'      manager_id=NULL
id=102 name='Ben (Manager)'  manager_id=101
id=103 name='Cara (Manager)' manager_id=101
id=104 name='Dev (Engineer)' manager_id=102
id=105 name='Ella (Engineer)' manager_id=102
id=106 name='Finn (Intern)'  manager_id=104
simpleExample.execution:
1. Base case runs once: finds employees with manager_id = 101 → Ben (depth 1), Cara (depth 1).
2. Recursive pass 1: joins employees against {Ben, Cara} → finds Dev and Ella (manager_id = 102 = Ben's id), depth 2.
3. Recursive pass 2: joins employees against {Dev, Ella} → finds Finn (manager_id = 104 = Dev's id), depth 3.
4. Recursive pass 3: joins employees against {Finn} → no employee has manager_id = 106, so zero rows are returned.
5. Zero new rows ends the recursion; all accumulated rows are returned by the outer SELECT.
simpleExample.output:
id=102 name='Ben'  depth=1
id=103 name='Cara' depth=1
id=104 name='Dev'  depth=2
id=105 name='Ella' depth=2
id=106 name='Finn' depth=3
simpleExample.explanation: Each recursive pass only looks one level deeper than the last, so the query naturally handles an org chart of any depth without hardcoding the number of levels. The `depth` column is carried forward and incremented each pass, giving a free by-product: how far down the chain each employee sits. The same shape works for a category tree by swapping `manager_id`/`employees` for `parent_id`/`categories`, and real systems typically add a depth cap or rely on the engine's recursion limit as a safeguard against a corrupted self-reference forming a cycle.

interviewExplanation: A CTE is a named, temporary result set defined with a WITH clause that you can reference like a table for the rest of that one statement. I'd reach for a non-recursive CTE mainly for readability — it lets me name each logical step of a query instead of nesting subqueries inside subqueries — and it disappears once the statement finishes, unlike a view.

A recursive CTE is the more interesting case: it has a base case that seeds the starting rows, and a recursive case that joins the CTE back to itself, re-running against only the newest rows each pass, until a pass produces nothing new. That's the standard tool for hierarchical data — an employee-manager chain or a category tree — where you don't know the depth ahead of time.

The thing I always mention is the stopping condition: recursion isn't driven by a loop counter, it stops automatically the moment a pass returns zero new rows. And in practice I'd guard it with a depth cap or lean on the engine's built-in recursion limit, in case the data has a cycle — like a manager_id that loops back on itself.

followUpQuestions.q: Why does a CTE exist when a subquery already does the same job?
followUpQuestions.a: Mainly readability and reuse. A subquery has to be re-written every place it's needed and reads inside-out when nested. A CTE gets a name, can be referenced multiple times in the outer query, and lets you chain several named steps top to bottom — which matters a lot once a query has more than one derived stage.

followUpQuestions.q: What happens internally when a recursive CTE runs?
followUpQuestions.a: The engine runs the base case once to produce the first generation of rows. Then it repeatedly runs the recursive member, but only against the rows produced in the immediately previous pass — not the whole accumulated set — unions the new rows in, and repeats. The moment a pass produces zero new rows, recursion stops and the full accumulated result is returned.

followUpQuestions.q: How is a recursive CTE different from a loop in application code?
followUpQuestions.a: A loop in application code means issuing a new query per level, round-tripping to the database each time and stitching results together yourself — slow and chatty. A recursive CTE expresses the same iteration declaratively inside one statement: the database handles seeding, iterating, and stopping internally, in a single round trip, and it can lean on set-based execution rather than row-by-row network calls.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the advantage of a CTE over a temp table?
followUpQuestions.a: A CTE needs no separate CREATE/DROP step and no disk or session storage — it's scoped to a single statement and disappears automatically. A temp table is heavier but persists across multiple statements in a session, which is genuinely useful when you need to reuse the same derived data across several separate queries, not just one.

followUpQuestions.q: What's a real disadvantage or gotcha with CTEs?
followUpQuestions.a: Materialization isn't guaranteed — some engines re-evaluate a non-recursive CTE every place it's referenced instead of computing it once, so assuming free reuse across a query can be wrong depending on the database and version. For recursive CTEs, the real risk is an unguarded cycle in the data causing runaway recursion until the engine's safety limit kills the query.

followUpQuestions.q: When would you avoid a recursive CTE?
followUpQuestions.a: When the hierarchy has a known, small, fixed depth — a few explicit JOINs are simpler and clearer than the base-case/recursive-case ceremony. I'd also be cautious using one on a very large, ungoverned hierarchy without a depth guard, since a data cycle can turn it into a runaway query.

bestPractices: Always include a way to stop runaway recursion — either a `WHERE depth < n` guard in the recursive member or rely on the engine's built-in recursion limit, and know what that limit is for your database.
Keep the recursive member's `SELECT` list column types identical to the base case's types (use explicit `CAST`) since most engines require matching types across the `UNION ALL`.
Use `UNION ALL` rather than `UNION` in the recursive step — deduplication is rarely needed and `UNION` forces an expensive distinct check on every pass.
Name CTEs descriptively (`active_customers`, not `cte1`) since the whole point is readability over a raw subquery.

When a non-recursive CTE is referenced many times and is expensive to compute, check whether your engine materializes it or re-evaluates it per reference — some databases offer an explicit `MATERIALIZED` hint (e.g., PostgreSQL) for this.
commonMistakes: Forgetting `RECURSIVE` in the `WITH` clause on engines that require it explicitly (PostgreSQL, MySQL 8+), causing a syntax error or the self-reference to be treated as an unrelated table.
Referencing the CTE more than once inside the recursive member's `FROM`/`JOIN` — most engines only allow a single reference to the CTE name per recursive step.
Using `UNION` instead of `UNION ALL`, which silently adds a large performance cost by deduplicating on every recursive pass.

Not guarding against cycles in the data (for example, an `employees` row where `manager_id` accidentally points back down the chain), which can cause infinite recursion until the engine's safety limit kills the query.
Assuming a non-recursive CTE always executes once and is cached automatically for reuse — this is engine- and version-specific behavior, not a guarantee of the SQL standard.
summary: A CTE names a subquery with `WITH name AS (...)` so the outer query can reference it like a table, improving readability without persisting anything to the schema.
A recursive CTE adds a base case and a self-referencing recursive case joined by `UNION ALL`, making it the standard declarative tool for hierarchical and graph-like data.
Recursion stops automatically once a pass produces zero new rows, so depth doesn't need to be known in advance.

relatedTopics.label: Window Functions — another tool for advanced result shaping

title: Window Functions
overview: A window function computes a value across a set of related rows — the **window** — and returns that value on every row in the set, without collapsing the rows into one, unlike `GROUP BY`.
The core syntax is `function() OVER (PARTITION BY col ORDER BY col)`, where `PARTITION BY` defines the window per group and `ORDER BY` defines the row sequence inside it.
